subtitle: Content strategy, positioning, article pipeline, and publication plan
outline:
# Medium Blog — Finaler Projektplan

**Datum:** 02.06.2026
**Status:** Vollständig — wartet auf Go/No-Go von Linus

---

## 0. Das Wichtigste zuerst: Du hast das alles schon gedacht.

Dein `acta/principles.md` (28. März 2026) enthält eine vollständige Content-Strategie:

> **Medium Publication: Applied AI**
> *"Explain AI in practical, understandable ways. Local AI vs. Cloud, Agent Systems, Industrial AI cases. Clear, non-academic language. No hype, just facts. Bridge theory → real-world application."*

> **Founder-Positionierung:** Engineering-first. Nordic-minimal. Transparent. Builder, not marketer.

Das ist der DNA-Strang. Wir müssen ihn nur aktivieren.

---

## 1. Was wir bauen: "Applied AI" — Eine Medium Publication

### Positionierung (3-Satz-Pitch)
Linus Kohl schreibt über den Bau souveräner KI-Systeme in einer Welt, die sich freiwillig in die Abhängigkeit von vier großen API-Anbietern begibt. Er tut dies mit echten Kosten-Daten, echter Infrastruktur und dem Blick eines Ingenieurs, der bereits erlebt hat, was passiert, wenn die Cloud ausfällt. Der Ton ist Economist: präzise, datengesättigt, meinungsstark — nicht belehrend.

### Zielgruppe
- Technische Entscheider (CTOs, Tech Leads in KMUs)
- Solo-Founder und Indie-Hacker
- Engineer-Typen, die "KI-Strategie" nicht von McKinsey erklärt bekommen wollen
- Leute, die ahnen, dass $200/Monat für ChatGPT Pro vielleicht nicht die Endstation ist

### Nischen-Claim
Während alle über GPT-5-Prompts schreiben, dokumentiert Linus **wie man KI-Souveränität operativ umsetzt** — mit Preisschild, Fehlschlägen und Architektur-Entscheidungen. Das ist keine "How to install Ollama"-Anleitung. Das ist ein War-Tagebuch eines Ingenieurs, der seine digitale Autonomie zurückerobert.

---

## 2. Redaktioneller Style Guide (Economist-Adaptiert)

### Prinzipien
| Regel | Umsetzung |
|-------|-----------|
| **30%-Regel** | Nach dem Schreiben 30% streichen. Jedes Wort muss kämpfen. |
| **Aktive Stimme** | "Ollama generated the response" — nicht "The response was generated by Ollama" |
| **Ein Gedanke pro Satz** | Keine Schachtelsätze. Variation im Rhythmus. |
| **Daten als Rückgrat** | Jede Behauptung mit Zahl belegen. "188M Tokens/Monat" nicht "sehr viele Tokens" |
| **Kurze Wörter** | "use" nicht "utilise", "cost" nicht "economic consideration", "built" nicht "architected" |
| **Keine Klischees** | Kein "game-changer", "paradigm shift", "democratize AI". Wenn es das nicht wirklich ist. |
| **Keine Didaktik** | Nicht "Consider the implications" — einfach die Implikationen nennen. |
| **Meinung ja, Arroganz nein** | Economist-Leser wollen wissen was du denkst, aber sie wollen nicht belehrt werden. |
| **Erzählung, nicht Lehrbuch** | Jeder technische Artikel erzählt eine Geschichte. Mit Spannungsbogen. |
| **Kein Namedropping** | Nenn Tools nur wenn sie für die Story relevant sind. |

### Format-Standards
- **Titel:** Klar, spezifisch, Neugier weekend. "How I Cut My AI Costs by 96% Without Sacrificing Quality" besser als "Ollama vs OpenAI: A Cost Comparison"
- **Lead:** Maximal 3 Sätze. Sag worum es geht und warum es wichtig ist.
- **Subheads:** Alle ~300 Wörter. Geben Struktur wie Wegweiser.
- **Absätze:** Maximal 4 Sätze. Weißraum ist dein Freund.
- **Schluss:** Keine Zusammenfassung. Ein Gedanke, der hängenbleibt.

### First-Person-Regeln
- **Artikel 1 (technisch):** Third-Person-Default, First-Person nur für Verankerung ("I ran this benchmark over 30 days")
- **Artikel 2 (narrativ):** First-Person dominant, weil die persönliche Reise die Story IST

---

## 3. Deine Antworten — Integriert

| Frage | Antwort | Implikation |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| 1. cabbagebaggage.net | Landing Page, sollte auf GitHub sein | Gefunden: `proper_landing/` + `landing_pages/`. ACTA hat cabbagebaggage als "Agent Interface Layer" definiert. Seite ist down (Cloudflare 523 Origin). → **Artikel 2-Aufhänger: "The page was down. The journey had already begun."** |
| 2. Startpunkt | Alter Desktop-PC, Ollama mit Phi und Llama | Git-Historie beginnt erst März 2026. Die eigentlichen Experimente waren vorher. → **Brauche von dir: Erinnerst du den ungefähren Monat (Nov/Dez 2025?)?** |
| 3. GitHub-Repos | Soll ich aus Commits lesen | Alle 6 repos sind 2026, aber Konzepte formten sich Ende 2025. Arca (Agentic ERP, GPLv3) ist das stärkste Narrativ. PhysIQ-App zeigt Produktentwicklung. |
| 4. Beruflicher Kontext | "Nicht so direkt" — Research + Industrie-Management | Strategie: Erwähnen als "an engineer who spent years in AI research before moving to industrial operations management". Kein Namedropping. |
| 5. Publication-Strategie | Eigene Publikation | **Entscheidung: "Applied AI" gründen** (wie in ACTA definiert). Parallel: Beste Artikel bei Towards Data Science / Better Programming pitchen. Das ist der Hybrid-Ansatz erfolgreicher Autoren. |
| 6. Monetarisierung | Thought Leadership + Side Income | Staffelung: Phase 1 (Monat 1–3) = Audience Building. Phase 2 (Monat 4–6) = MPP + Affiliate. Phase 3 (Monat 7+) = Consulting-Signale. |
| 7. Frequenz | 1–2 Artikel/Monat | Realistisch. Launch mit 2 Artikeln gleichzeitig, dann 1 alle 2–3 Wochen. |
| 8. OpenClaw-Repo | Kann erwähnt werden, ggf. Public-Fork | Strategie: Public-Fork mit Article-Referenz erstellen. "The configuration files I discuss are available on GitHub" → Link. |

---

## 4. Was große Medium-Publisher als Alternative wären

| Publikation | Follower | Vorteil | Nachteil |
|-------------|----------|---------|----------|
| **Towards Data Science** | 800K+ | Größte Data-Science-Pub, perfekte Zielgruppe | Lange Review-Zeiten, hohe Ablehnungsquote |
| **Better Programming** | 300K+ | Developer-fokussiert, gute SEO | Kein AI-Fokus |
| **The Startup** | 800K+ | Entrepreneurship/Indie-Hacker | AI-Content muss Business-Winkel haben |
| **Level Up Coding** | 200K+ | Engineering-Kultur | Weniger AI-affin |

**Empfehlung:** Gründung "Applied AI" + Pitchen bei TDS nach 3–4 eigenen Artikeln (Social Proof).

---

## 5. Monetarisierungspfad (realistisch)

```
Monat 0–1   │ Launch, 2 Artikel, 0 Follower
            │ LinkedIn-Posts, Hacker News (wenn relevant)
Monat 2–4   │ 3–4 weitere Artikel, erste TDS-Pitches
            │ MPP-Aktivierung, erste $10–50/Monat
Monat 5–8   │ 100–500 Follower, Email-Liste starten
            │ Affiliate-Links (Hostinger, Ollama, Docker-Bücher)
Monat 9–12  │ 500–1.500 Follower, regelmäßige Einnahmen $50–200/Monat
            │ Erste Consulting-Anfragen ("Can you help us set this up?")
Jahr 2      │ 2.000–5.000 Follower, Sponsorship-Anfragen
            │ ACTA-Website als Conversion-Punkt
```

**Vergleichbare Erfolgsfälle:**
- **Simon Willison** — Datenjournalist/Engineer, baute Nische um "AI-assisted development", Monetarisierung via Consulting + Talks
- **Eugene Yan** — Dokumentiert ML-System-Design, Monetarisierung via Buch + Consulting
- **Lilian Weng** (OpenAI) — Technische Deep-Dives, Monetarisierung via Karriere-Positionierung

---

title: Headline Analysis — Applied AI Publication
subtitle: Headline testing methodology, A/B variants, and performance analysis for technical content
outline:
# Headline Analysis v2 — "Applied AI" Medium Publication

**Date:** 2026-06-06  
**Format:** Popular-scientific paper (Abstract → Introduction → State of Technology → Methodology → Results → Discussion → References)  
**Tone:** Nature/Science for educated general audience — Economist precision, no clickbait

---

## Scoring Criteria

Weighted for scientific paper format — credibility and clarity outweigh curiosity:

| Criterion | Weight | Definition |
|-----------|--------|------------|
| **CTR** — Click-Through Potential | 0.25 | Would a technical reader click? Curiosity gap without cheap tricks |
| **CLR** — Clarity | 0.20 | Do you know exactly what you'll learn in under 2 seconds? |
| **ECN** — Economist Fit | 0.20 | Precision, no hype, no buzzwords, let data speak |
| **SCI** — Scientific Credibility | 0.15 | Would this headline appear in Nature/Science/ACM? Gravitas |
| **SEO** — SEO Value | 0.10 | Search-friendly keywords, discoverability |
| **NCH** — Niche Appeal | 0.10 | Speaks to technical decision-makers, engineers |

---

## Article 1 — Technical Deep-Dive: Multi-Model AI Routing

**Topic:** 188M tokens/month, 96% via free Ollama Cloud, 39.5x ROI (€3,988 value on €101 spend). Ollama Cloud vs OpenAI Codex vs Claude API vs DeepSeek API. Fallback chains, routing architecture.

### Full Scoring Table

| # | Headline | Subtitle | CTR | CLR | ECN | SCI | SEO | NCH | **Σ** |
|---|----------|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| A1 | **Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit** | Real-world cost and performance analysis across four providers — 188M tokens, €101 total spend | 7 | 10 | 9 | 10 | 8 | 9 | **8.70** |
| A2 | **The Economics of Self-Hosted AI Inference** | 188 million tokens, four model providers, and the architecture that eliminates 97.5% of API costs | 7 | 9 | 10 | 10 | 8 | 9 | **8.70** |
| A3 | **Reducing AI Inference Costs by 97.5% Through Tiered Model Routing** | A production case study from 30 days of multi-provider traffic on OpenClaw | 8 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 8 | **8.70** |
| A4 | **An Empirical Comparison of AI Model Providers Under Production Load** | Ollama Cloud, DeepSeek, Claude, and OpenAI Codex — 188M tokens analyzed | 6 | 10 | 10 | 10 | 8 | 9 | **8.65** |
| A5 | **Token Economics: The Real Cost of Multi-Provider AI Infrastructure** | How a tiered routing strategy delivered 39.5× ROI and ended single-vendor dependence | 7 | 9 | 10 | 9 | 8 | 9 | **8.60** |
| A6 | **Free AI at Production Scale Is a Routing Problem, Not a Quality Problem** | 188M tokens per month, 96% on zero-cost infrastructure — here is the architecture | 8 | 8 | 8 | 9 | 7 | 8 | **8.10** |
| A7 | **The Case Against Single-Vendor AI: Evidence from 188 Million Tokens** | Why multi-provider routing outperforms any single API on cost, reliability, and sovereignty | 8 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 8 | 9 | **8.30** |
| A8 | **Operational Costs of AI Agent Infrastructure: A Quantitative Analysis** | Measuring and optimizing the true cost of large-scale AI inference across four providers | 6 | 9 | 10 | 10 | 8 | 8 | **8.30** |
| A9 | **Zero-Cost AI Inference: Architecture and Empirical Validation** | How Ollama Cloud, DeepSeek, and tiered fallbacks delivered 188M tokens at €101/month | 7 | 8 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 8 | **8.10** |
| A10 | **AI Model Routing in Practice: Costs, Latency, and Failure Modes** | Lessons from a 30-day production deployment handling 188 million tokens across four backends | 7 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 8 | **8.35** |

### Article 1 — Top 3

#### 🥇 A1: "Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit" — 8.70

**Why it wins:**
- Maximum clarity (10) and scientific credibility (10). Reads like an ACM or USENIX paper.
- "Production Audit" signals empirical rigor — this is measured, not opinion.
- Subtitle delivers all key numbers: 188M tokens, €101, four providers.
- Economist fit (9): dry, factual, no superlatives.
- "Multi-Model AI Routing" is the precise technical term — discoverable and accurate.

**Risk:** Lower CTR (7) than punchier alternatives. Mitigated by niche audience who self-selects for rigor.

#### 🥈 A2: "The Economics of Self-Hosted AI Inference" — 8.70

**Why it's tied:**
- Maximum Economist fit (10) and scientific credibility (10). Reads like an Economist briefing.
- "Economics" frames this as a strategic decision, not a tutorial — attracts decision-makers.
- Strong niche signal: anyone who cares about inference economics is the exact right reader.
- "Self-Hosted" in the title is a high-intent search term.

**Risk:** Slightly less specific in the title than A1. Subtitle compensates.

#### 🥉 A3: "Reducing AI Inference Costs by 97.5% Through Tiered Model Routing" — 8.70

**Why it's in the top 3:**
- Highest CTR (8) among the top candidates — "97.5%" is a concrete, credible claim.
- Strong SEO (9): "AI Inference Costs" and "Model Routing" are high-value keywords.
- Scientific credibility (9): the exact percentage and mechanism are stated upfront.
- "Tiered Model Routing" names the actual technique — not "trick" or "hack."

**Risk:** Slightly less prestigious than A1/A2. The percentage could read as "growth hack" if not handled carefully.

### Article 1 — Final Recommendation

**Primary: A1** — "Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit"

This is the strongest fit for the scientific paper format. It signals empirical rigor from the first word. The subtitle carries the narrative weight. For a reader scanning Medium, "Production Audit" immediately separates this from tutorial content.

---

## Article 2 — Narrative Journey: Digital Sovereignty

**Topic:** Paternity leave (Jan 2026) → digital independence. Ollama + Phi/Llama on old desktop → Docker containers → OpenClaw on Hostinger VPS → Oracle Free Tier → Ollama Cloud. cabbagebaggage.net. ACTA philosophy.

### Full Scoring Table

| # | Headline | Subtitle | CTR | CLR | ECN | SCI | SEO | NCH | **Σ** |
|---|----------|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| B1 | **Toward Digital Sovereignty: One Engineer's Migration from Cloud AI to Self-Hosted Infrastructure** | How paternity leave became a three-month project to reclaim control over AI compute — and why the architecture matters | 7 | 8 | 10 | 9 | 8 | 9 | **8.40** |
| B2 | **From API Customer to Infrastructure Owner: A Personal Case Study in AI Sovereignty** | Three months, a newborn, an old desktop, and the decision to stop renting intelligence | 8 | 8 | 9 | 8 | 7 | 9 | **8.25** |
| B3 | **The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure** | Lessons from building a self-hosted, multi-model AI system during paternity leave | 7 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 9 | **8.45** |
| B4 | **AI Autonomy as an Architectural Principle** | How a series of experiments with local models led to a production multi-provider system — and the philosophy behind it | 6 | 7 | 9 | 9 | 7 | 8 | **7.65** |
| B5 | **Local AI: From Experiment to Infrastructure** | The evolution from Ollama on a desktop to 188M tokens/month across four model providers | 7 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 8 | **8.35** |
| B6 | **Digital Agency Through Self-Hosted AI: A Personal Account** | What building your own AI infrastructure reveals about dependence, cost, and control | 7 | 7 | 10 | 9 | 7 | 8 | **7.95** |
| B7 | **Infrastructure as Declaration: Building AI Systems Outside the Cloud** | From a dusty desktop PC to a four-model production router — one engineer's path to digital independence | 8 | 7 | 9 | 9 | 7 | 8 | **8.10** |
| B8 | **The Sovereignty Stack: Reclaiming AI Compute Through Self-Hosting** | A personal journey from OpenAI customer to infrastructure owner, told through architecture decisions | 7 | 8 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 8 | **8.15** |
| B9 | **What a Server You Control Means in the Age of API Monoculture** | Reflections on paternity leave, an old desktop, and the architectural case for digital sovereignty | 8 | 6 | 9 | 8 | 6 | 7 | **7.50** |
| B10 | **Self-Hosted AI at Production Scale: Motivation, Architecture, and Implications** | The personal and technical journey from a single desktop experiment to a multi-provider AI router | 6 | 9 | 9 | 10 | 8 | 9 | **8.30** |

### Article 2 — Top 3

#### 🥇 B3: "The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure" — 8.45

**Why it wins:**
- Maximum Economist clarity (9): exactly what the article argues, stated plainly.
- Natural scientific credibility (9): "The Case for X" is a classic academic essay format.
- "Personal AI Infrastructure" names a concept that barely exists in public discourse — this article would define it.
- Strong niche appeal (9): every engineer who has thought "I should probably self-host something" is the reader.
- Subtitle grounds it in the personal narrative without the title needing to.

**Risk:** Lower CTR (7) — less curiosity gap. Mitigated by the abstract, which will carry the emotional hook.

#### 🥈 B1: "Toward Digital Sovereignty: One Engineer's Migration from Cloud AI to Self-Hosted Infrastructure" — 8.40

**Why it's close:**
- Maximum Economist fit (10): measured, precise, no emotion in the title itself.
- "Migration" is the right verb — signals a process, not an epiphany.
- Strong subtitle tells the personal story.
- "Digital Sovereignty" is a rising search term.

**Risk:** Longer title (16 words). Marginal — still scannable. More impersonal than B3.

#### 🥉 B5: "Local AI: From Experiment to Infrastructure" — 8.35

**Why it's in the top 3:**
- Excellent clarity (9): you know the arc immediately.
- "From X to Y" is a proven academic title structure — signals evolution, not opinion.
- Compact (7 words). Scannable.
- "Local AI" is the exact search term for this audience.

**Risk:** Slightly generic. Doesn't say what KIND of infrastructure. Subtitle compensates.

### Article 2 — Final Recommendation

**Primary: B3** — "The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure"

This positions the article as an argument grounded in evidence, not a diary entry. It defines a term. It makes the personal journey structural — the data and experience support a thesis, rather than the thesis serving as an excuse to tell a story. For a scientific paper format, this is the right frame.

---

## Combination Strategy

### Recommended Pairing: A1 + B3

| | Article 1 (Technical) | Article 2 (Narrative/Philosophical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Headline** | Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit | The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure |
| **Format role** | The empirical paper — proves feasibility | The position paper — argues necessity |
| **Reader journey** | "Here is evidence this works." | "Here is why you should care." |
| **Publication order** | First — establishes technical credibility | Second — builds on data with argument |
| **Narrative function** | The numbers | The story behind the numbers |

### Publication Order & Timing

1. **Day 1: Article 1** — "Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit"
   - Establishes Applied AI as a publication that leads with data.
   - Gives Article 2 something to reference: "As I documented in the production audit..."
   - Appeals to the widest technical audience.

2. **Day 4–5: Article 2** — "The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure"
   - Builds on the credibility of Article 1's data.
   - Broadens the argument from "this works" to "this matters."
   - Personal narrative lands harder when the reader already trusts the author's rigor.

---

## v3 Hybrid: Bridging Scientific Credibility and Medium CTR

### The Problem

v1 headlines optimized for Medium's algorithm (8.45, 8.10). v2 headlines optimized for scientific gravitas (8.70, 8.45). But the CTR loss is real:

- **Article 1 v1→v2:** Estimated 20–30% fewer clicks. Numbers (€101, 188M) buried in subtitle. "Production Audit" is dry.
- **Article 2 v1→v2:** Estimated 35–45% fewer clicks. Personal story ("my son learned to sleep") completely removed. "The Case for…" reads like a policy brief.

Medium's feed CTR averages ~3%, with >5% considered good. Numbers, personal hooks, and "How I" patterns consistently outperform declarative/academic titles. Subtitles help but don't fully compensate.

### The v3 Hybrid Approach

Keep the scientific structure (Abstract → Discussion) but restore numbers and personal context to the headline. The title signals intellectual seriousness; the hook drives the click.

### v3 Headlines

**Artikel 1:**
> **What 188 Million Tokens of AI Traffic Actually Cost — A 30-Day Production Audit**
>
> *Real-world cost and performance analysis across four model providers — €101 total spend, 39.5× ROI*

**Artikel 2:**
> **The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure — Built During Paternity Leave**
>
> *Lessons from a three-month project that turned an old desktop and a newborn into a declaration of digital independence*

### Scoring (same criteria, v3 headlines)

| # | Headline | CTR | CLR | ECN | SCI | SEO | NCH | **Σ** |
|---|----------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|-------|
| A1-v3 | What 188 Million Tokens of AI Traffic Actually Cost — A 30-Day Production Audit | 9 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 8 | 9 | **8.95** |
| B3-v3 | The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure — Built During Paternity Leave | 8 | 9 | 9 | 9 | 7 | 9 | **8.55** |

### v3 vs v2 vs v1 Comparison

| Article | v1 (Click) | v2 (Scientific) | v3 (Hybrid) | CTR Loss v1→v3 |
|---------|-----------|-----------------|-------------|----------------|
| Article 1 | 8.45 | 8.70 | **8.95** | ~10% |
| Article 2 | 8.10 | 8.45 | **8.55** | ~15% |

v3 scores higher than both v1 and v2 because it captures both dimensions:
- The number (188M) and personal context ("Built During Paternity Leave") that drive clicks
- The scientific framing ("Production Audit", "The Case for…") that signals intellectual seriousness
- The dash structure creates a headline + qualifier rhythm without the weakness of colons

**Estimated net CTR loss vs purely click-optimized v1: 10–15%.** Worth it for the positioning gain.

---

## Final Refined Recommendations (v3 — Applied)

### Article 1 — Final Headline

> **Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit**
>
> *Real-world cost and performance analysis across four providers — 188 million tokens, €101 total spend*

**Rationale:** A1 scored highest (8.70) on the scientific-weighted matrix. It signals empirical rigor, names the technique precisely, and lets the subtitle carry the narrative hook. This is a paper, not a blog post.

**Alternative if Linus wants slightly more warmth:** A3 — "Reducing AI Inference Costs by 97.5% Through Tiered Model Routing" (also 8.70, higher CTR).

### Article 2 — Final Headline

> **The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure**
>
> *Lessons from building a self-hosted, multi-model AI system during paternity leave*

**Rationale:** B3 scored highest (8.45). It defines a new concept category, positions the article as an argument grounded in evidence, and allows the deeply personal material (fatherhood, the old desktop, cabbagebaggage.net) to emerge within the body rather than the headline. The subtitle bridges the formal title to the human story.

### Headline Style Rules (Applied AI Publication)

All future articles in this publication should follow these headline rules:

1. **Lead with the finding or concept, not the story.** The title is the thesis.
2. **No colons unless the first clause is a complete thought.** "Toward Digital Sovereignty: One Engineer's…" is fine. "AI Routing: A Study" is too light.
3. **Specific numbers over general claims.** "97.5%" beats "dramatically." "188M tokens" beats "at scale."
4. **Avoid gerunds as the first word.** Not "Building AI Infrastructure" — instead "The Case for…" or "Toward…"
5. **The subtitle carries the emotional or personal dimension.** The title carries the intellectual payload.
6. **No questions in titles.** The paper format answers, it doesn't ask.

---

## Appendix: Full Scoring Breakdown

### Weight Distribution (Scientific Format)

| Criterion | Weight | Rationale |
|-----------|--------|-----------|
| CTR | 0.25 | Even serious papers need readers. Scientific credibility ≠ invisibility. |
| CLR | 0.20 | Precision of communication is the core value of good writing. |
| ECN | 0.20 | The publication's editorial identity. Non-negotiable. |
| SCI | 0.15 | New criterion for the paper format. Signals intellectual seriousness. |
| SEO | 0.10 | Search matters, but never at the expense of the above. |
| NCH | 0.10 | Engineers self-select. Broad appeal is not the goal. |

### Comparison: v1 (Click-Optimized) vs v2 (Scientific Paper Format)

| Article | v1 Winner | v1 Score | v2 Winner | v2 Score | Shift |
|---------|-----------|----------|-----------|----------|-------|
| Article 1 | "The €101 AI stack that handles 188 million tokens per month" | 8.45 | "Multi-Model AI Routing: A 30-Day Production Audit" | 8.70 | +0.25 |
| Article 2 | "The AI infrastructure I built while my son learned to sleep" | 8.10 | "The Case for Personal AI Infrastructure" | 8.45 | +0.35 |

**Key observation:** The scientific format's emphasis on credibility lifts both scores. The v1 headlines optimized for Medium's algorithm; v2 headlines optimize for a publication with an intellectual identity. The trade-off is real: lower raw CTR potential, but higher trust and differentiation in a market saturated with "How I X" content.
